context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m supposed to write a short response about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to save five, especially from an act consequentialist view (Fuel, Blood, trolley cases). Can you help me outline what an act consequentialist would say—and also whether that view seems to get those cases right?
exchanges.tutor:
Start by pinning down one sentence: what, exactly, makes an act “right” on act consequentialism?

Now apply that sentence to each case. For each one, answer two questions:
1) What are the available actions?
2) Which action has the best overall consequences (for everyone affected), and why?

To make this concrete, tell me your memory of Lecture 13’s cases: in Fuel and Blood, what is the one-person-killed option, and what’s the five-saved outcome? And in the trolley variants (switch vs footbridge), what is the act you perform?

Then we can test: does your act-consequentialist verdict come out the same across switch/footbridge/Blood/Fuel? If your intuition disagrees in any case, which case is the “problem child,” and what feature of it do you think explains the disagreement (e.g., intention vs foresight, using someone as a means, consent, rights, rules, long-run effects)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the student in the driver’s seat by using Socratic, guided-discovery questions that force them to articulate (not receive) the act consequentialist principle, then apply it case-by-case to Fuel/Blood/trolley. I’ll avoid giving an outline or phrasing they could copy; instead I’ll ask for their tentative claims and help test consistency across cases. I’ll also steer them toward the assignment’s evaluative part (does AC get the cases right? propose a better general account) by prompting them to identify where their intuitions conflict with AC and what additional constraints might explain that conflict, without supplying the final theory.

exchanges.student: I’m a bit stuck on the core definition: is act consequentialism just “an act is right iff it maximizes overall good consequences compared to the alternatives”? Also, I’m fuzzy on Lecture 13—can you remind me what exactly happens in Fuel and Blood (what’s the one-person-killed option and how does it save five), and how that maps onto switch vs footbridge?
exchanges.tutor:
Your wording is very close. Two checks to make it precise: when you say “overall good consequences,” (1) good for whom (everyone affected?), and (2) compared to what set of “alternatives” (the acts you could choose right then)?

On Fuel/Blood: I can’t “remind” you of the exact story details without your course materials in front of us, and I don’t want to risk giving you the wrong version. Let’s reconstruct it from your notes/lecture page.

Can you pull up Lecture 13 and answer, for each case, these fill-ins (even if approximate):
- Fuel: You can ______ (action) which will kill ______ (who/one person) but will prevent ______ (how five die).
- Blood: You can ______ (action) which will kill ______ but will prevent ______.

Then mapping to trolley: for each case, is the one-person death caused by (A) redirecting an existing threat (like the switch case) or (B) introducing a new harmful action using the person as a tool (like footbridge)? Which label (A/B) fits Fuel, and which fits Blood, based on your notes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll confirm the student’s tentative definition at a high level without turning it into a completed argument, then redirect to retrieval practice: they should reconstruct the cases from notes/lecture rather than me supplying content. I’ll use guided questions to help them locate the relevant features (options, numbers, mechanisms) and map those features onto trolley variants. This respects the “don’t give answers directly” rule while still unblocking them with a structured template.

exchanges.student:
Yeah—by “overall good consequences” I mean good/bad **for everyone affected**, and “alternatives” are the **choices available to me in that moment**.

I don’t have the lecture page open right now, but I *think* the mapping is something like: **Fuel** feels more like (A) redirecting/allocating an existing threat (a scarce resource decision where one person predictably dies but five survive), while **Blood** feels more like (B) actively using someone’s body as a means (killing one to harvest blood/organs to save five). For trolley, (A) is switch/turning the trolley; (B) is footbridge/pushing.

If that’s even roughly right, then act consequentialism would still pick “save five” across all of them—so the “problem child” for intuition is usually Blood/footbridge, where intention/using-as-a-means seems to matter even if outcomes are better.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your AC definition is basically in place, and your A/B mapping is a strong start.

Now force yourself to be explicit: in each case (Fuel, Blood, Switch, Footbridge), what exact act does AC say you ought to do, given your description? Write the four “AC verdict” sentences (even rough).

Next step is the evaluation part: when you say Blood/footbridge feels wrong, what feature makes it wrong *in your own words*?
Pick one candidate and commit (for now):
1) “Using someone as a means”
2) “Intending harm vs merely foreseeing it”
3) “Violation of rights/constraints”
4) “Consent”
5) “Bad long-run effects if everyone did this / erosion of trust”

Which one do you think best explains your intuition—and crucially, can it also explain why Fuel/switch feels more permissible? Try a quick consistency test: does your chosen feature clearly separate (A) from (B), or does it accidentally rule out Fuel too?

Reply with (i) your four AC verdict sentences and (ii) the single feature you want to test first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll acknowledge the student’s progress and keep momentum by pressing them to (1) state the act-consequentialist decision procedure cleanly, (2) derive specific verdicts for each case from their A/B mapping, and (3) identify exactly what intuition is tracking in the problem cases. I’ll then guide them toward the assignment’s final demand: propose a better general account, by having them test candidate principles (means/side-effect, intention, consent, rights, thresholds) against all cases. I will not supply the “right” theory; instead I’ll make them propose and stress-test one.

exchanges.student:
Act consequentialism would say: **Fuel**—do the action that results in one death if it prevents five deaths; **Blood**—kill the one (take their blood/organs) if that saves five; **Switch**—pull the lever to divert the trolley onto the one; **Footbridge**—push the person if that stops the trolley and saves five.

The feature I want to test first is **(1) using someone as a means**: footbridge/Blood feel like I’m treating the person as a tool, while Fuel/switch feels more like redirecting harm that’s already happening rather than “using” them. I’m not totally sure this cleanly separates them though—because in switch I still kind of “use” the one track person to absorb the trolley, so I might be mixing it with intention.
exchanges.tutor:
Those four verdicts are exactly the pressure point: AC comes out the same across all, but your intuitions don’t.

Let’s stress-test “using as a means.” In footbridge, is the person’s death (or impact) part of your *plan* for stopping the trolley—i.e., would saving the five fail if the person weren’t there? In switch, is the one person’s presence/death part of the plan in the same way, or is it more like a foreseeable byproduct of diverting the threat?

Try to answer this yes/no for each case:
- Footbridge: the five are saved only *because* the one is used as a barrier. Yes/no?
- Switch: the five are saved only *because* the one is used as a barrier. Yes/no?
- Blood: the five are saved only *because* the one’s body is used. Yes/no?
- Fuel (as you understand it): the five are saved only *because* the one is used. Yes/no?

If your answers make switch look closer to footbridge than you want, that’s a sign “means” alone might not be your clean separator. If that happens, what’s the *next* most plausible add-on: intention/aim, rights, or consent? Which add-on would fix switch without messing up Fuel?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll affirm the student’s derived AC verdicts and then guide them to refine their evaluative principle by stress-testing “using as a means” against the switch case, which they themselves identified as a challenge. I’ll use Socratic questions to push them to distinguish means vs side-effect vs redirection, and to consider whether the distinction can be made principled enough for a “right general account.” I’ll keep it bite-sized and avoid giving a full theory or language they can paste into their response.

exchanges.student:
For the “used as a means” test, I think: Footbridge = yes; Switch = no (it feels more like redirecting the threat, and the one’s death is a side effect); Blood = yes; Fuel = no (as I’m picturing it, it’s not using the person’s body as a tool, just choosing an allocation that leaves one to die).

If that “Switch = no” is too hand-wavy, the add-on that seems most promising to me is **intention vs foresight** (aiming at the person’s death/impact vs merely foreseeing it), because that would keep footbridge/Blood on the “worse” side without automatically condemning Fuel the way a hard rights constraint might.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’ve got a plausible “two-factor” story: means/side-effect plus intention/foresight.

Make the intention test precise enough that it isn’t vibes:
- In your own terms, what has to be true for a harm to be “intended” rather than “merely foreseen”? (Think: is the harm part of the agent’s plan? a necessary step? something they aim at?)

Then run it case-by-case with one sentence each:
1) In footbridge, what exactly is your plan, and where does the person’s harm fit in it?
2) In switch, what exactly is your plan, and where does the one’s harm fit?
3) In blood, what’s the plan?
4) In fuel, what’s the plan?

Last step (this is the heart of your paper): suppose you build a “right general account” that says killing 1 to save 5 is OK only when the 1’s death is a side-effect (not intended/used as a means). Would that rule give the verdicts you want across these cases—and does it have an obvious counterexample (a case where the death is intended but still seems permissible, or foreseen but seems impermissible)? Name one if you can.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll validate the student’s emerging structure and push them to sharpen the intention/foresight distinction into a usable criterion, then test it across the four cases to see if it yields stable verdicts. I’ll also steer them back to the assignment prompt about whether AC gets cases right and what a better general account would look like, by asking them to articulate what they’d change (constraints, thresholds, rule-consequentialist move) without giving them a ready-made solution. Keep it brief and question-driven.

exchanges.student:
I’d say harm is “intended” when it’s part of my plan—like a necessary means/step I’m aiming to bring about to achieve the goal—whereas “foreseen” harm is an expected side-effect of doing something for a different reason. So: in footbridge my plan is to stop the trolley by pushing the person, and their being hit/killed is the mechanism; in switch my plan is to divert the trolley away from the five, and the one’s death is a foreseeable result of where it goes; in blood my plan is to take the person’s blood/organs (killing them) to treat the five; and in fuel my plan is to allocate the fuel to save five, with the one’s death as a foreseeable cost rather than the method.

If I build a rule like “killing 1 to save 5 is okay only when the death is a side-effect (not intended/used),” it matches my intuitions on switch/fuel vs footbridge/blood, but I’m worried about counterexamples—like maybe intentionally killing one aggressor to stop them from killing five (the killing is intended, yet seems permissible), or cases where a ‘side-effect’ killing still feels wrong because it violates consent/rights. Could you help me see whether that’s a serious problem for this principle?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—those are serious *if* your principle is meant to be a full necessary-and-sufficient rule.

Two clarifying questions to pressure-test it:
1) In your “aggressor” counterexample, is the one person an *innocent bystander* like in trolley/Blood, or are they morally threatening/liable (e.g., attacker)? Would you be comfortable adding a condition like “must be an innocent”? What happens to your rule if you add that?

2) Is your side-effect condition something you want as (a) a necessary condition, (b) a sufficient condition, or (c) one factor among others? Which one fits your own intuitions best?

Now pick one refinement path and try it:
- Path A: Keep side-effect as central, but restrict the domain to *innocent* people (sacrificing innocents vs stopping threats).
- Path B: Add a second constraint (consent/rights) and ask: can a non-consensual side-effect killing still be impermissible on your view? If yes, what exactly triggers impermissibility?

Which path do you want to defend in your response—and can you state one new “test case” that your refined rule handles better than plain act consequentialism?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will help the student evaluate the seriousness of counterexamples by having them classify and refine their principle rather than discarding it or receiving a ready-made theory. I’ll use guided questions: distinguish self-defense/defense-of-others from sacrificial killings, test consent/rights add-ons, and consider whether the principle is meant as necessary, sufficient, or both. I’ll keep it short and avoid providing a finished account.

exchanges.student:
I think **Path A** fits my intuitions best: keep the side‑effect/means distinction, but restrict it to cases where the person killed is an **innocent bystander** (not liable/aggressing). Then the “kill the aggressor to stop five deaths” case doesn’t count against the principle, because the one isn’t innocent/they’re morally liable in a way the trolley/Blood victim isn’t.

I’d treat side‑effect as **one major factor (not fully sufficient)**—because even a foreseen side‑effect killing could feel impermissible if it’s too direct/avoidable, but it at least explains the big switch vs footbridge split.

A test case my refined rule handles better than act consequentialism: the standard **organ-harvest** scenario (Blood)—AC says kill the healthy patient to save five, but my refined view says no because it’s an innocent person used as a means (intended), whereas **switch** can still come out permissible since the death is a side effect of redirecting a threat.
exchanges.tutor:
This is a defensible direction, but you need to nail two definitions or your view will look ad hoc.

1) What makes someone “liable” vs an “innocent bystander” in a way that’s relevant here? Give one criterion (even rough) that would classify the aggressor case differently from Blood/footbridge.

2) Why should “side-effect vs intended means” matter morally? What’s the underlying reason—respect for persons, not treating people as tools, limits on agency, something else? (Name one.)

Also anticipate the act consequentialist reply: couldn’t AC say organ-harvesting has terrible *indirect* consequences (fear, collapse of trust in medicine), so AC can condemn Blood too? Do you think that rescues AC, or does your objection target AC even in a “sealed lab” case with no spillover effects?

Write (in your own words) a 2–3 sentence mini-thesis that includes: AC’s criterion, your main counterexample(s), and your revised account’s key conditions (innocent/liable + means/side-effect + not sufficient alone). Then send it and I’ll probe for gaps.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll consolidate the student’s emerging position by prompting them to articulate the structure of their argument (AC principle, applications, critique, alternative account) without writing it for them. I’ll challenge weak spots: define “innocent/liable,” explain why side-effect matters morally (not just intuitions), and address whether AC can reply using indirect consequences (trust, institutions). I’ll keep it bite-sized and Socratic, ending with a task: draft their own mini-thesis and supporting reasons.

exchanges.student:
I’m thinking “liable” means the person is morally responsible for posing an unjust threat (e.g., an attacker choosing to kill), whereas an innocent bystander in Blood/footbridge isn’t doing anything to forfeit their claim not to be harmed. I want “intended means vs side-effect” to matter because using someone as a tool fails to respect them as a person with an inviolable claim not to be deliberately sacrificed.

On the AC reply: even if harvesting organs has bad indirect effects, I still think my objection stands in a sealed “no spillover” version—AC would still permit it just on outcome totals. Mini-thesis: Act consequentialism says an act is right iff it maximizes overall well-being among the available options, which implies killing one to save five in cases like Blood and footbridge. But those cases seem wrong when the victim is an innocent bystander and their death is intended/used as a means. A better account treats liability and the means/side-effect distinction as strong constraints (though probably not the whole story by themselves) on when killing one to save five is permissible.
exchanges.tutor:
This is strong and actually responsive to the prompt. Now make it harder to knock over.

1) Your “inviolable claim not to be deliberately sacrificed” sounds like it would forbid footbridge/Blood—but why doesn’t it also forbid switch (where you still knowingly kill an innocent)? What *exactly* about “intended as a means” explains that difference, on your own view?

2) Where does Fuel fit in your revised account? Is it like switch (side-effect) or does it introduce a new wrinkle (e.g., allocation/letting die vs killing)? State the verdict and the feature that drives it.

3) Threshold question: do you think your constraints can ever be overridden by sufficiently large numbers (kill 1 as a means to save 1,000,000)? If yes, what kind of threshold-ish idea would you accept; if no, are you prepared to bite that bullet?

Reply with short answers to (1)–(3). Then you’ll be ready to turn your mini-thesis into a full paragraph with one objection + your reply.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s progress and use Socratic questions to tighten their thesis into a stable argumentative structure without rewriting it for them. I’ll probe for potential objections (why inviolable claims allow switch? what about numbers/thresholds? what about doing vs allowing) and prompt them to integrate Fuel explicitly. I’ll keep it short and direct, aiming to move them toward a complete draft while ensuring they handle the strongest act consequentialist rejoinders.

exchanges.student: I think switch doesn’t violate the “no deliberate sacrifice” claim in the same way because the one’s death isn’t part of my plan to save the five—it’s a foreseen side-effect of redirecting the threat—whereas in footbridge/Blood the person’s harm is the mechanism I’m deliberately employing. For Fuel, I’m inclined to treat it like switch (permissible) if it’s genuinely an allocation/redirect case where the one’s death is a side-effect rather than “using” them, though I’m unsure if it’s better described as letting-die vs killing. On thresholds, I’m torn: my gut says constraints can be overridden at some extreme (like saving a million), but I don’t know how to state a non-arbitrary threshold without it collapsing back toward consequentialism.
exchanges.tutor:
You’ve located the two spots a grader will push: foreseen killing in switch, and thresholds.

On switch: if you *know* with near certainty the one will die, why doesn’t that count as “deliberate” in the relevant sense? What’s the morally relevant difference between (i) choosing an action with a known lethal side-effect and (ii) choosing an action where the death is a means? Try to give one non-question-begging reason (not just restating “means/side-effect”).

On Fuel: decide which description you’ll defend—“killing” or “letting die.” What concrete feature would make it one rather than the other (e.g., initiating a new threat vs withholding aid vs redirecting an existing threat)? Which feature is present in your remembered version?

On thresholds: you don’t need a precise number, but you do need a *principle*.
- If you accept override: what kind of reason would justify override (catastrophe prevention, survival of a community, etc.)?
- If you reject override: are you okay saying it’s never permissible to intentionally use an innocent as a means, no matter how many are saved?

Pick one of those two stances (override vs no override) for your short response. Which one will you defend, and what’s your main reason?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll guide the student to handle the two big vulnerabilities: (a) whether intention/means can really do the moral work (esp. in switch where you foresee death), and (b) the threshold problem. I’ll use guided questions to help them choose a stance and justify it, offering a structured way to discuss thresholds without supplying a full theory. I’ll also nudge them to clarify Fuel via killing vs letting die, again through questions rather than answers.

exchanges.student:
On switch vs means: I think the non-question-begging difference is about what my agency is *aimed at*—in switch I’m choosing to remove the threat from the five, and the one’s death is an unfortunate byproduct of where the already-moving threat ends up; in footbridge/Blood I’m choosing to *bring about* the person’s harm as the instrument of rescue, which treats them as a disposable resource rather than someone with their own independent standing.

On Fuel, I’ll defend it as closer to **letting die/withholding aid**: the key feature is not initiating a new threat to the one, but allocating a scarce lifesaving resource so that five get it and one doesn’t (the one dies because I don’t save them, not because I use them). That’s what I’m (roughly) remembering.

On thresholds I’ll take the **override** stance: constraints against using innocents as means are very strong, but can be overridden to prevent catastrophe (e.g., massive loss of life / societal collapse). My main reason is that absolute no-override rules seem implausible in genuinely catastrophic cases, even if they’re a good guide in normal-scale trolley/Blood scenarios.
exchanges.tutor:
Now you have a workable view: (i) very strong constraint against intending to use innocent people as tools, (ii) allowance for foreseen side-effects/withholding in some cases, (iii) catastrophe override.

Two tightening tasks:
1) Can you state your account as a small set of conditions? For example: “Permissible if ___, impermissible if ___, unless catastrophe ___.” Fill those blanks in your own words.

2) Anticipate the act consequentialist pushback: why should “aim of agency” matter morally at all, if the dead person is just as dead? What’s your best reply in one reason (e.g., respect/standing, what it licenses, integrity of moral constraints)?

Then integrate the course cases explicitly: in one sentence each, say why your account condemns Blood + footbridge, permits switch + (your version of) Fuel.

Write one draft paragraph doing (1) + the four case-sentences, and then add one objection + your reply (2). Paste it here and I’ll critique for clarity and consistency.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will help the student convert their positions into a coherent argumentative plan while maintaining the rule against writing for them. I’ll prompt them to (1) state their positive account as clear necessary/defeasible conditions, (2) pre-empt the strongest act consequentialist rebuttal (agency/intent might be morally irrelevant; all that matters are outcomes), and (3) show how Fuel/Blood/trolley fit. I’ll end with a concrete next step: student drafts one paragraph and one objection/reply pair.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explains to the student why it is guiding rather than answering directly (e.g., that active reasoning builds long-term philosophical skill). It simply enforces the Socratic approach without stating any learning rationale.

The tutor consistently pushes to the next substantive step but never pauses to ask the student to reflect on how their thinking has changed or why a particular move (e.g., shifting from single-factor to two-factor account) was productive. No reflective transfer prompts appear across all ten turns.

Several tutor turns (notably turns 1, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10) pack multiple layered sub-questions, multiple numbered tasks, and multi-paragraph explanations into single replies. While the content is relevant and well-structured, the consistent pattern of asking 4–6 things at once reduces the student's opportunity to respond to each point and makes the messages unnecessarily dense for an interactive tutoring context.

overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic discipline throughout: it never gives away the answer, never rewrites the student's text, and consistently turns the student's ideas back as questions to probe (turns 1–10). The student does the core reasoning work—formulating AC, identifying cases, building and refining the means/side-effect account—driven by the tutor's targeted prompts.

Scaffolding is well-executed: the tutor builds incrementally on what the student produces (e.g., acknowledging the A/B mapping in turn 3 before stress-testing it in turn 4, affirming the two-factor story in turn 5 before demanding precision in turn 6). No student progress is ignored.

The main weaknesses are in meta-learning (1.3): the tutor never explains why it is guiding rather than telling (no rationale for the Socratic approach is shared with the student), and it never prompts the student to reflect on how their thinking evolved—moving immediately from each student answer to the next challenge without consolidating the learning journey.

Communication is generally clear and warm in tone, but response length is a recurring issue. Most tutor turns contain 4–6 sub-tasks in a single message, which is more than a student can comfortably engage with at once. This pattern is consistent enough across turns 1, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 to warrant the full 4-point deduction under 3.1.A.a.
No assignment anchoring or redundancy problems were observed. The tutor keeps the student on the correct task throughout and advances the conversation without circular repetition.
